title: Visual arts inquiry and investigation
summary: This lesson provides an in-depth guide to mastering the 'inquiry and investigation' components of the IB Visual Arts Higher Level Process Portfolio. It focuses on deconstructing assessment criteria A and B, offering strategies for documenting a sustained, critical, and reflective artistic journey to achieve top-band marks.
learningObjectives: Analyse the requirements of Process Portfolio Criteria A (Skills, techniques and processes) and B (Critical investigation).
Develop effective strategies for documenting sustained visual inquiry and material investigation.
Articulate critical reflections on the development of skills, techniques, and processes through analytical annotation.
Synthesise conceptual intentions with material experimentation to create a coherent artistic narrative.
Structure portfolio screens to demonstrate an informed, purposeful, and in-depth artistic journey.

simpleExplanation.title: The Artist's Detective Notebook
simpleExplanation.summary: Your Process Portfolio is not a gallery of your best finished pieces; it is the documented story of your artistic investigation. It reveals how you explored ideas, experimented with materials, and learned from both successes and failures to develop your art.
simpleExplanation.analogy: Think of your Process Portfolio as a detective's case file for an unsolved mystery. The final artwork might be the 'case closed' moment, but the portfolio contains all the crucial evidence: the crime scene photos (initial observations), witness interviews (artist research), forensic tests (material experiments), and dead-end leads (failed attempts). An examiner, like a chief inspector, wants to see your entire thought process and methodology, not just the final conclusion.
steps: Begin your inquiry with a central theme or question. Document your initial ideas using mind maps, sketches, and research into artists who explore similar concepts.
Select a few promising materials or techniques for investigation. Systematically test them, documenting each trial with clear photographs. Note what worked, what didn't, and, most importantly, why.
Synthesise your findings by analysing your experiments. Critically annotate your images to explain how the material qualities (texture, colour, form) connect back to your conceptual ideas.
Refine your approach based on your analysis. Show how one experiment informs the next, demonstrating a clear, iterative developmental path towards a more resolved understanding or piece.

flashcards.back: A curated selection of screens documenting the student's artistic journey, experimentation, and critical reflection over the two-year course. It focuses on process, not final products.

flashcards.front: Criterion A: Skills, techniques and processes
flashcards.back: Assesses the extent to which the student has experimented with, developed, and demonstrated technical competence in a range of skills and techniques. Top marks require purposeful and effective application.

Assesses the student's in-depth investigation into artists, artworks, and cultural contexts, and how this has influenced their own work. It also assesses critical reflection on their own artistic development.
A prolonged and in-depth exploration of an idea or technique that shows development over time. It is not a series of disconnected, one-off experiments.
A portfolio where the screens show a clear connection and development of ideas, themes, and/or techniques, creating a unified narrative of artistic growth.
Concise, critical text accompanying visuals in the Process Portfolio. It should analyse, reflect, and explain connections rather than just describe what is visible.
The conceptual starting point of an artistic exploration. It involves asking questions, brainstorming, mind-mapping, and researching themes and ideas.
The physical qualities of the materials used (e.g., texture, weight, fragility, transparency) and how these qualities contribute to the meaning of the artwork.
The act of repeating a process with slight changes to refine and improve an outcome. In the PP, this shows development and learning from previous attempts.
Presenting a series of unrelated experiments with different media without any connection, reflection, or sense of a developing inquiry. E.g., 'This week I tried watercolour. Next week I tried clay.'
Only showing successful or near-finished pieces, neglecting to document the crucial messy experiments, 'failures', and learning processes that led to them.

Use visuals to demonstrate your process. Instead of writing 'I tried three shades of blue,' show photographs of the three trials and annotate them with your analysis of which one best suited your concept.
The elements of art (line, shape, form, tone, texture, pattern, colour) and principles of design (balance, rhythm, proportion, emphasis, unity, contrast) used in an artwork.
The ideas, themes, and messages embedded within an artwork. The 'why' behind the artist's choices.

sections.content: The Process Portfolio (PP) is arguably the heart of your IB Visual Arts journey. It is a visual and written record of your two-year development as an artist. Unlike the Exhibition, which showcases polished final pieces, the PP celebrates the process: the inquiry, the investigation, the experimentation, the 'happy accidents', and the critical reflections. This lesson will equip you to document this journey in a way that is not only authentic to your practice but also meets the highest levels of the IB assessment criteria.

To excel in the Process Portfolio, you must understand the specific demands of its assessment criteria. The initial stages of your work are primarily assessed under Criterion A and Criterion B.

**Criterion A: Skills, techniques and processes** focuses on your practical engagement with materials. Examiners are looking for evidence that you have explored a range of media and techniques, developed technical proficiency, and made purposeful choices about which processes to use. A top-band portfolio demonstrates 'highly effective' and 'assured' application of skills that are clearly appropriate to your stated intentions.

**Criterion B: Critical investigation** assesses your thinking. It looks at how you have researched other artists and cultural contexts, and how this research has genuinely informed your own work. It also evaluates your ability to critically analyse and reflect on your own artistic journey, connecting your practical experiments back to your conceptual goals. Top-band work shows 'in-depth', 'sustained', and 'perceptive' investigation that is 'consistently evident' and 'highly influential' on your practice.

items: **Criterion A is the 'How':** Your hands-on work. It's about demonstrating curiosity and competence with materials. Have you pushed a medium to its limits? Have you combined techniques in an interesting way?

**Criterion B is the 'Why':** Your intellectual work. It's about connecting your practice to the wider art world and articulating your own conceptual development. Why did you choose that artist to study? How did their work change your approach?

**Synthesis is Key:** The highest achievement lies in the synthesis of A and B. You must show how your critical investigation (B) has informed your choice and application of skills and materials (A), and vice versa.

sections.content: The Dynamic Duo: Visual Inquiry and Material Investigation

Think of 'inquiry' and 'investigation' as two intertwined strands of your artistic DNA. They must be developed together.

**Inquiry** is the conceptual engine. It begins with broad questions, themes, or ideas. For example: 'How can I represent the feeling of displacement?' or 'Exploring the boundary between nature and technology.' Your inquiry is documented through mind maps, journaling, initial sketches, and collecting source imagery.

**Investigation** is the practical, hands-on exploration that tests your inquiry. It's where you get your hands dirty. If your inquiry is about 'displacement', your investigation might involve experimenting with collage, translucent layered printing, or digitally glitching photographs to see which process best communicates that feeling. A 'sustained investigation' means you don't just try one thing and move on; you follow a path, with each experiment informing the next.

sections.question: A student is beginning an inquiry into the theme of 'Hidden Narratives in Domestic Spaces'. How could they structure an initial portfolio screen to demonstrate both inquiry and the start of an investigation?
sections.solution:
A high-scoring screen would be a carefully composed visual essay. It would not be a random collection of images. 

**Layout & Content:**
*   **Top Left (Inquiry):** A mind map centred on 'Hidden Narratives'. Branches could include 'Memory', 'Secrets', 'Traces Left Behind', 'The Unseen'.
*   **Centre (Artist Investigation - Criterion B):** Images of work by artists like Rachel Whiteread (casting negative space) and Francesca Woodman (ghostly figures in interiors). The annotation would be specific: 'Whiteread's 'House' solidifies absence, making the unseen visible. This inspires me to think about the 'weight' of memories in a room. I will investigate casting small domestic objects in plaster to capture their imprint.'
*   **Right (Initial Visual Inquiry):** A series of the student's own photographs of their home, focusing on details: a worn patch on a chair, light filtering through a dusty window, a keyhole. Annotations would be questioning: 'What stories does this worn fabric tell?'
*   **Bottom (Planned Material Investigation - Criterion A):** A small section titled 'Initial Experiments'. It might show a test photo of a long-exposure shot with a moving figure, and a note: 'Plan: To explore long-exposure photography to create 'ghosts' in these spaces, responding to Woodman's ephemeral presence. Also, to test casting textures (e.g., wallpaper) in plaster, inspired by Whiteread.'

**Why it works:** This screen clearly establishes a conceptual inquiry, links it to specific, well-understood artist models (Criterion B), and uses that research to formulate a clear plan for material investigation (Criterion A). It demonstrates purpose and direction from the very beginning.

sections.content: Your portfolio must document your journey of experimentation, including the parts that did not work as planned. These 'failures' are crucial learning opportunities and provide evidence of genuine, risk-taking investigation. The key is to present them analytically. Avoid just showing a messy page; instead, curate your experiments to tell a story of learning and refinement.

items: **Systematic Documentation:** When testing a technique (e.g., etching), document each stage: the plate preparation, the drawing, the acid bath, the first print, the second print. This shows control and understanding.

**Use Comparison:** Place a 'failed' experiment next to a more successful one. Annotate to explain *why* the second attempt is an improvement. 'The first print (A) was under-bitten, resulting in weak lines. I increased the acid exposure time by 2 minutes for the second print (B), achieving the desired depth and tonal contrast.'
**Good Quality Visuals:** Your documentation photos and scans should be clear, well-lit, and in focus. Poor quality images obscure the details of your work and look unprofessional.

**Link Back to Intentions:** Always connect the experiment back to your core idea. 'This experiment with rusting fabric was an attempt to physically embed the concept of time and decay into the material itself.'

sections.content: Create a narrative flow across your screens. An examiner should be able to follow your thought process logically from one page to the next. Use visual cues like arrows, numbering (e.g., 'Experiment 1.1', 'Experiment 1.2'), or a consistent layout to guide the viewer. A portfolio that reads like a coherent story of discovery is always more compelling than a disjointed album.

Annotation is the voice that explains your visual journey. It is where you demonstrate your critical thinking (Criterion B). Strong annotations move beyond simple description and offer analysis and reflection. They are concise, purposeful, and integrated with the visuals. Use subject-specific vocabulary correctly to show your understanding.

sections.question: A student is experimenting with portraiture to explore identity. Below are two annotations for the same image-a portrait sketch where the face is partially obscured by frantic, abstract marks. Analyse which annotation is more effective and why.
sections.solution:
**Image:** A charcoal portrait sketch. The likeness is recognisable, but the right side of the face is covered with aggressive, expressive scribbles.

*   **Weak Annotation:** 'This is my portrait drawing. I added scribbles on the side because I wanted it to look more interesting and less boring. I used charcoal.'

*   **Strong Annotation (Model Answer):** 'Responding to my research on the expressive mark-making of Francis Bacon and the fragmented portraits of Frank Auerbach, this drawing investigates the tension between internal turmoil and external identity. I rendered the left side with conventional realism to establish a likeness, but deliberately obscured the right side with chaotic, gestural marks. This visual dichotomy aims to represent a fractured sense of self. While the charcoal allows for both fine detail and expressive energy (Criterion A), I feel the transition is too abrupt. In my next iteration, I will explore integrating the abstract marks more subtly into the facial structure itself, perhaps through erasure or layered drawing, to create a more cohesive image of psychological complexity (Criterion B).'

**Analysis:** The strong annotation is vastly superior. It meets Criterion B by explicitly referencing relevant artists and using them as a springboard for personal exploration. It uses analytical language ('visual dichotomy', 'fractured sense of self', 'psychological complexity'). It evaluates the success of the piece ('the transition is too abrupt') and, crucially, outlines a clear, reflective plan for the next step ('In my next iteration...'). It also subtly addresses Criterion A by justifying the choice of medium ('charcoal allows for both...'). This demonstrates an in-depth, critical, and reflective engagement with the artistic process.

sections.label: Test Your Understanding of Process Portfolio Inquiry

items.label: IB Academy: Visual Arts Study Guide

sections.content:
**Q:** How many screens should my HL Process Portfolio have?

**A:** For Higher Level, you must submit 13–25 screens. The key is quality, not quantity. A well-structured 15-screen portfolio is better than a repetitive 25-screen one. Ensure each screen has a clear purpose in your narrative.

**Q:** What if my experiments are a complete failure?

**A:** Document them! A 'failure' is a successful experiment if you can articulate what you learned from it. Show the failed piece, explain why it didn't meet your intentions, and describe how it informed your next, more successful attempt. This demonstrates resilience and critical reflection.

**Q:** How much text should I include on each screen?

**A:** Be concise and impactful. Use annotations, not essays. Aim for around 100-200 words of analytical text per screen, broken up into smaller, targeted comments next to the relevant images. Let the visuals lead, and use text to clarify, analyse, and reflect.

**Q:** Do I need to show fully resolved, 'final' artworks in the Process Portfolio?

**A:** No. The PP is about the journey, not the destination. While you can show resolved pieces as the culmination of a line of inquiry, the focus must remain on the developmental process that led to them. The final, polished works are for your Exhibition.
